sources.name: Wikipedia — Castlevania: Bloodlines
sources.notes: Two-character system. John Morris whip mechanics, Eric Lecarde spear mechanics confirmed.

sources.name: Speedrun.com — Castlevania Bloodlines
sources.notes: Speedrun community documentation including spear vault and grapple mechanics.

notes: Castlevania: Bloodlines is the ONLY classic-era Castlevania on the Genesis (vs NES/SNES Castlevanias).
TWO PLAYABLE CHARACTERS: John Morris (whip, Belmont descendant) and Eric Lecarde (spear, new character).
ERIC'S SPEAR VAULT: Eric can plant his spear on the ground and use it to vault to extreme heights — a vertical traversal tool without parallel in the series.
ERIC'S GRAPPLE: Eric can hook his spear to specific surfaces for swinging traversal.
John's whip plays like classic Castlevania — committed jump arc, whip for combat.
The two characters have different jump physics — Eric's spear vault creates a super-high jump alternative.
World War I setting — unique among the Castlevanias (Europe 1917).

john_morris._note: Belmont-lineage character. Classic Castlevania whip gameplay.

jump.notes: Classic Castlevania committed jump. No air control mid-arc.

vampire_killer_whip.description: Same Vampire Killer whip as Simon Belmont. Standard Castlevania whip physics.
notes: Whip extends horizontally forward. Can hit enemies slightly above/below with angled positions.
Classic Castlevania whip — the same fundamental tool used across the series.

john_morris.notes: John Morris plays identically to the classic Castlevania template — familiar to series veterans.

eric_lecarde._note: NEW character type. Spear creates fundamentally different traversal options.

jump.notes: Standard jump same as John. The spear vault provides the exceptional height.

spear_attack.description: Eric thrusts his spear forward. Different hitbox from John's whip — longer reach, different arc.
spear_attack.notes: Spear has slightly different timing than whip — thrust animation vs swing animation.

spear_vault._note: THE DEFINING MECHANIC. Creates extreme vertical height.
spear_vault.description: While on the ground, Eric can plant his spear and vault upward — launching himself to a height far exceeding the standard jump.
notes: The vault height is dramatically higher than normal jump — allows reaching elevated platforms John can't.
The vault creates vertical routing options specific to Eric.
Landing from vault height requires careful platform placement — missing the target creates a long fall.

spear_grapple.description: Eric can hook his spear onto specific grapple points in levels for pendulum-style swinging.
notes: Similar concept to Simon's grapple whip in Super Castlevania IV.
The grapple creates horizontal traversal across gaps.
The combination of vault (vertical) and grapple (horizontal) gives Eric a complete aerial toolkit.

character_selection.description: Player chooses John or Eric before the game begins. Different character = different routes in some areas.
notes: John's committed jump + whip: classic Castlevania feel, familiar to series veterans.
Eric's spear vault + grapple: new traversal toolkit, allows reaching different areas.
Some speed routes are only possible with Eric (vault to skip sections). Others favor John's whip range.
The two characters create replay value — playing both reveals different paths through the same level.
World War I European setting. Stages include Versailles, Pisa, Greece, Romania, Transylvania.
6 stages total.
Bloodlines notably features two stages (Pisa Tower) that use the Genesis' ability for parallax scrolling with unusual angles.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Eric Lecarde's spear vault is the most dramatic movement expansion in classic Castlevania — a character who can reach heights impossible for the whip user. The height differential creates genuine character differentiation: not 'more damage' or 'more speed' but 'reaches different places.' Spatial differentiation.

The John/Eric pairing demonstrates 'same world, different physics.' Both characters traverse the same stages with the same tile maps, but Eric's vault creates vertical shortcuts John can't access. The level design accommodates both without requiring it — skillful play with either character.

Bloodlines' World War I setting distinguishes it from every other Castlevania. The visual contrast (WWI Europe vs medieval fantasy) is jarring but creates a unique identity. Same mechanics, different aesthetic frame — the mechanics transfer across historical settings.

The grapple mechanic (Eric's spear hook) used in a committed-jump Castlevania creates interesting tension: grappling changes the trajectory mid-air in a series defined by committed arcs. The grapple is a controlled deviation from the arc constraint — a precision tool within a constraint system.

Bloodlines' obscurity (Genesis-only, never re-released until SNES-era collections) means many players never experienced it. The Eric Lecarde character specifically represents an unexplored design branch — a Castlevania with both classic and completely new traversal tools, largely unseen.
